evidence_quotes.quote: videogames becoming unplayable after the publisher pulls support.
Should be sold as a subscription. Where a certain amount of money is paid for a number of months/years of playability.
deceptive licence that might last forever or 1 month.

paragraphs.text: I would like to add to the issue concerning videogames becoming unplayable after the publisher pulls support. Whilst it is reasonable that they cannot support every game forever if this is a burden. And that burden is not self inflicted and only there so they can destroy the capability for the game to be played. As to move players on to newer titles. It is unreasonable that at the moment of purchase, where in all language at the point of sale it is indicated that it is a purhcase, not a rental, there is no information on how long the game will be working for. Therefore, if publishers want to have the right to discontinue a game. I believe those games, that are currently under a deceptive licence that might last forever or 1 month. Should be sold as a subscription. Where a certain amount of money is paid for a number of months/years of playability. During which the game or servers cannot be closed down. Though of course the prefence would be for games to release so that they could be run in private servers or fully on consumer hardware where possible. Even if a somewhat technical person needs to do the work for the community. I would also like to request games, as a form of art to be saved, stored, in some manner where they can be accessed by researchers, game developers and the wider public in a museum like setting. So that the art form, the talent, the ideas are not lost when the support for the game is ended.
